name: Wide Area Network — SD-WAN with Azure ExpressRoute
approvers: Blair (Director, IE&O); Quintin Grant (Sr Manager, Enterprise Architecture, IE&O)
strategicIntent: Establish a secure, resilient, and scalable Wide Area Network across all McCain Foods sites using Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN as the primary branch interconnect fabric and Azure ExpressRoute as the dedicated private connectivity path to cloud-hosted datacenters. This pattern replaces legacy DMVPN and Aliant Ethernet circuits with a software-defined overlay that enables centralized policy management, performance-based routing, and seamless multi-site integration. The architecture positions McCain Foods to adopt AI/ML-driven AIOps and multi-cloud optimization in future fiscal years.
problemStatement: McCain Foods operates 180+ global manufacturing sites and office locations requiring reliable, secure inter-site connectivity that legacy technologies such as Cisco DMVPN over internet and Aliant/Bell Ethernet circuits can no longer adequately support at scale. These legacy approaches lack centralized policy control, offer limited failover agility, and create operational complexity that drives up cost and risk. The organization needs a modern WAN architecture that supports cloud-first datacenter migration to Azure, integrates security services inline, and provides a programmable foundation for future AIOps and multi-cloud capabilities.

useCases.use: Connecting branch manufacturing sites to corporate datacenter resources and Azure-hosted workloads via SD-WAN overlays with integrated secure internet gateway tunnels (Zscaler).
useCases.dontUse: Small isolated sites or temporary deployments where SD-WAN hardware provisioning lead times or licensing costs are disproportionate to the connectivity requirement.

useCases.use: Providing dedicated, private, high-bandwidth connectivity between McCain Foods on-premises datacenters and Azure regions using Azure ExpressRoute during and after datacenter migration.
useCases.dontUse: Internet-only workloads or SaaS applications that do not require private peering and are better served directly through Zscaler or other cloud security proxies.

useCases.use: Replacing legacy Cisco DMVPN and Bell/Aliant Ethernet circuits at branch locations with a unified SD-WAN fabric that supports HA configurations and internet backup circuits.

components: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN — Primary WAN overlay fabric for branch and Azure connectivity; software-defined policy management across all sites
Cisco Catalyst 8000 Series Edge Platforms — Next-generation edge routers deployed at branches and hub sites; sized to site requirements; 'Invest' quadrant hardware
Cisco ISR 1000 Series Routers — Supported branch routers for smaller sites; sized appropriately to connectivity requirements
Azure ExpressRoute — Dedicated private circuit connecting on-premises datacenters to Azure regions; eliminates public internet path for datacenter-to-cloud traffic
Zscaler Secure Internet Gateway — Cloud-delivered security proxy integrated with SD-WAN branch deployments via IPSec/GRE tunnels for secure internet breakout
Alternative Internet Backup Circuits — Secondary internet connections providing WAN resilience at branch sites; planned investment item
Cisco SD-WAN Security Integration (IDS/IPS, Firewall) — Inline security services being assessed for integration into the SD-WAN fabric in Fiscal 27
AI/ML-driven AIOps — Intelligent network operations and performance-based routing capability on the SD-WAN roadmap for Fiscal 27–28
interactionSteps: Step 1 — Branch site edge router (Cisco Catalyst 8000 or ISR 1000) establishes SD-WAN overlay tunnels to SD-WAN controllers and hub nodes across available WAN transports (internet circuits).
Step 2 — SD-WAN fabric enforces centralized application and traffic policies, performing application-aware routing and quality-of-service across all branch-to-branch and branch-to-datacenter paths.
Step 3 — Internet-bound traffic from branch sites is redirected via Zscaler Secure Internet Gateway tunnels for cloud-delivered security inspection before egressing to the internet.
Step 4 — Datacenter-to-Azure traffic traverses Azure ExpressRoute private peering, bypassing the public internet and providing predictable, low-latency connectivity for migrated workloads.
Step 5 — SD-WAN orchestration layer monitors path health and performs automated failover between primary WAN transport and backup internet circuits when degradation is detected.
Step 6 — Centralized SD-WAN management plane (vManage) provides visibility, policy enforcement, and configuration lifecycle management across all sites and edge devices.
Step 7 — Future: AI/ML-driven AIOps layer ingests telemetry from SD-WAN fabric to provide predictive analytics, anomaly detection, and automated performance-based routing decisions.
securityConsiderations: All SD-WAN overlay tunnels must use strong encryption (IPSec with AES-256) to protect data in transit across untrusted internet transport circuits.
Zscaler Secure Internet Gateway integration is mandatory for all branch internet breakout; direct internet access without security proxy inspection is not permitted.
Azure ExpressRoute private peering must be used for datacenter-to-Azure connectivity; public peering over internet must not be used for datacenter workload traffic.
SD-WAN security integration (IDS/IPS and integrated firewall) is on the investment roadmap and must be evaluated and deployed as part of the Fiscal 27 planning cycle.
SD-WAN controller plane (vManage/vSmart/vBond) must be access-controlled with MFA and RBAC; unauthorized access to the controller plane represents a single point of policy compromise.
Network segmentation must be maintained between IT and OT environments; SD-WAN fabric must not bridge OT segments without explicit security review and compensating controls.
Legacy DMVPN and Aliant Ethernet circuits must be decommissioned on schedule to eliminate parallel attack surfaces during the transition period.
Vendor and third-party access to SD-WAN management plane must be governed by least-privilege principles and logged for audit purposes.

guardrails.rule: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN must be the sole WAN overlay fabric for all inter-site connectivity; Cisco DMVPN is sunset and must not be provisioned for new or replacement circuits.
Cisco ISR 4000 Series routers are sunset (N-1); no new procurement of 4000 Series is permitted. Replacement deployments must use Catalyst 8000 Series or ISR 1000 Series as appropriate.
Bell/Aliant Ethernet circuits are sunset (N-1) and must be transitioned to Cisco SD-WAN over internet transport; no new Aliant Ethernet circuits may be contracted.
Azure ExpressRoute must be used for private datacenter-to-Azure connectivity; internet-routed connectivity to datacenter workloads in Azure is not permitted.
All branch internet-bound traffic must be routed through Zscaler Secure Internet Gateway tunnels; direct internet breakout without inspection is prohibited.
Edge router model selection (Catalyst 8000 vs ISR 1000) must be sized appropriate to site bandwidth, redundancy, and service requirements; a site capacity assessment must precede hardware selection.
Branch deployments should implement High Availability (HA) configurations with redundant edge devices and backup internet circuits where site criticality and budget permit.
Any deviation from approved WAN technology standards must follow the SOE exception and approval process; no unapproved WAN technologies may be deployed.
New WAN investments must align to the published SOE Quadrant (Support/Invest); technologies in Watch or Sunset quadrants must not receive new capital investment without SOE owner approval.

exceptionProcess: All exceptions to the WAN technology standard must be reviewed by the SOE Owner (Network Architect) in consultation with relevant SMEs, Network Engineers, and Support Staff. The exception request is then circulated to designated approvers (IE&O Director and Sr Manager, Enterprise Architecture) for formal sign-off before any non-standard technology is deployed. Approved exceptions must be documented in the SOE and tracked for remediation alignment with the next roadmap review cycle.

revisionHistory.author: AI Assistant
revisionHistory.summary: Initial APL entry derived from McCain Foods WAN SOE v3.0 (Dec 12, 2025), capturing SD-WAN and Azure ExpressRoute architecture pattern, SOE quadrant positioning, roadmap intent, and guardrails.
